metadata.title: Package It Like a Product: Exec Summary + Architecture Diagram
metadata.playlist: Make It Smart — Scale Your Madison Intelligence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 4 brief — Scale Your Thing & Add Intelligence

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 6 of 7. You have a working workflow, a gallery of outputs, and a scale test report. Now you turn that into materials a hiring manager would actually look at. Two pages in Figma: an executive summary and a technical architecture diagram. The executive summary answers: what does it do and why does it matter? The architecture diagram answers: how does it actually work, and what happens when it fails? Both require real numbers and real failure paths — not aspirational claims. Missing the Figma board costs fifteen points. Let's build it right. Let's go.

props.segment: Package It Like a Product: Exec Summary + Architecture Diagram
props.command: Build 2 Figma pages: exec summary (what + why + results) and architecture diagram (how + failure paths).
props.runningText: missing Figma board = -15 pts; real metrics beat aspirational claims…
output: Exec Summary: problem · solution · sample outputs · metrics · business value · badge
Architecture diagram: data flow · AI components labeled · failure paths from E2
Design: hierarchy, headings — not a sticky-note pile

beats.narration_text: The executive summary is one Figma page that a hiring manager can read in ninety seconds. Six sections. Problem: two sentences describing the job the workflow does and who it does it for. This is not your A2 problem statement — it is a translation of that problem into language a business stakeholder understands. Solution: three to five bullets naming what the workflow does, starting with the most important capability. Sample outputs: one or two screenshots from your gallery, cropped and labeled. Results: three to four numbers from the scale test and quality section — actual numbers, not ranges. Process time per record, quality rate, cost per hundred requests. Business value: one sentence on what this workflow saves or produces for someone in the target role. And a 'Built with n8n and [your AI service]' badge — a small text label, not a logo, naming the tools. The badge is a portfolio signal: it tells the reader you used real production tools, not just described them.

artifactLines: Problem: 2 sentences — what the workflow does and for whom (business language, not A2 phrasing).
Solution: 3–5 bullets — key capabilities, most important first.
Sample outputs: 1–2 gallery screenshots, cropped and labeled.
Results: 3–4 real numbers — process time per record · quality rate · cost per 100 requests.
Business value: 1 sentence — what this saves or produces for the target role.
Badge: 'Built with n8n + [AI service]' — portfolio signal, not decoration.
props.sparkLine: Real numbers. Business language. Readable in 90 seconds.

beats.narration_text: The architecture diagram is one Figma page showing how data moves through the workflow and what happens at every decision point, including failures. Four requirements for the diagram. First: data flow. Show every node in the workflow as a labeled box. Draw arrows showing the direction of data flow from one node to the next. Label the arrows with what kind of data moves along them — 'clean records,' 'AI classification JSON,' 'HTML output file.' Second: AI components labeled. The AI node should be visually distinct — a different color or shape — and labeled with the AI service name and what it does: 'OpenAI gpt-4o-mini — sentiment classification.' Third: integration points. Where does external data come in? Where does output go? Label those entry and exit points. Fourth: failure paths. For every On-Error branch you built in Episode 2, show the branch in the diagram as a dashed line leading to a box labeled with what happens — 'log error + continue' or 'write to failed-records log.' A diagram without failure paths tells the grader the error handling was added to the documentation but not to the design.

artifactLines: 1. Data flow: labeled boxes (nodes) + arrows with data-type labels between them.
2. AI components: visually distinct (color/shape) + labeled with service name + task.
3. Integration points: labeled entry (data in) and exit (output destination) points.
4. Failure paths: dashed lines from each error branch → labeled with what happens.
No failure paths = error handling added to docs but not to design.
props.sparkLine: Flow. AI labeled. Integrations. Failure paths. All four.

beats.narration_text: The presentation excellence points are comparative — the best-presented boards in the class score higher, and this is one of the most visible comparison points. Three things that make the Figma board look professional rather than thrown together. First: visual hierarchy. Title at the top, section labels in a slightly larger or bolder weight, body text consistent. Use Figma's text styles if you have not already — set one style for headings and one for body text, then apply them throughout. Second: alignment. Use Figma's alignment and distribution tools so every element lines up. A layout where boxes are slightly off-axis or text floats in different positions reads as rushed. Third: whitespace. Leave room between sections. A dense layout where everything is packed together is harder to read than one with deliberate spacing. You are not trying to fit as much information as possible on the page — you are trying to make the most important information readable at a glance. The hiring manager skims this in the first ten seconds. The first thing they see is structure. Make the structure legible.

artifactLines: 1. Visual hierarchy: title → section labels → body text. Use Figma text styles for consistency.
2. Alignment: use align/distribute tools — off-axis elements read as rushed.
3. Whitespace: space between sections. Dense = hard to read. Sparse = easy to skim.
Hiring manager skims in 10 seconds. First thing they see: structure.
Goal: most important info readable at a glance — not maximum information per page.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Package It Like a Product. Missing the Figma board costs fifteen points. Executive summary: six sections — problem in business language, solution bullets, sample outputs, real results numbers, business value, and a built-with badge. Architecture diagram: labeled boxes, arrows with data-type labels, AI node visually distinct, integration points marked, failure paths as dashed lines. Every failure path from Episode 2 must appear in the diagram — a diagram without them tells the grader the error handling was documentation-only. Design: hierarchy, alignment, whitespace. The goal is a layout that a hiring manager can skim in ten seconds and understand what was built. Real metrics beat aspirational claims. A diagram that shows failure paths beats one that only shows the happy path. Episode 7 is the demo and the submission.

props.artifactTitle: Package It Like a Product: Exec Summary + Architecture Diagram
artifactLines: Missing Figma board = -15 pts.
Exec summary: problem · solution · sample outputs · real numbers · business value · badge.
Architecture: labeled boxes + arrow labels + AI node distinct + integration points + failure paths.
No failure paths in diagram = error handling was docs-only.
Design: hierarchy · alignment · whitespace. Readable at a 10-second skim.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude to draft the executive summary text before you lay it out in Figma. The prompt reads: I am writing an executive summary for a Figma board about an n8n workflow. The workflow does this: [one sentence describing the core AI capability]. It processes this type of data: [describe your A3 data]. My A2 target role was: [one sentence]. Here are my real performance numbers: quality rate [percentage], process time per record [seconds], cost per hundred AI requests [dollar amount], total outputs generated [number]. Write the six sections of the executive summary as formatted text I can paste into Figma: Problem in two sentences using business language, Solution as three to five bullets, a Results section with those four metrics formatted as a two-column table, Business value in one sentence, and the badge line 'Built with n8n + [AI service]'. After you paste the text into Figma, build the architecture diagram manually — do not attempt to auto-generate it. The act of drawing it is the act of verifying that you understand how the data moves.

props.segment: Package It Like a Product: Exec Summary + Architecture Diagram
props.command: Workflow does: [one sentence]. Data: [describe]. Target role: [one sentence]. Numbers: quality rate [%] · time per record [sec] · cost per 100 requests [$] · outputs generated [N]. Write the 6 exec summary sections for Figma: Problem (2 sentences, business language) · Solution (3–5 bullets) · Results (4 metrics as 2-column table) · Business value (1 sentence) · badge.
props.runningText: draw the architecture diagram manually — that act verifies you understand the data flow…

beats.narration_text: Package It Like a Product: Exec Summary + Architecture Diagram. Liam, in for Bear.
